title: Lemon Posset
subtitle: Double cream set with lemon juice, no gelatine required
excerpt: Three ingredients: double cream, caster sugar, and fresh lemon juice. The acid in the lemon sets the cream without any gelatine, the mixture is cooked briefly, the lemon juice is stirred in, and the whole thing sets firm in the fridge within two hours. One of the simplest desserts in this collection.
sourceNotes: The posset has medieval English roots as a warm drink of hot milk curdled with wine or ale and spices, taken as a remedy. The modern lemon posset is a much later development, a cold set cream using the acid in lemon juice to coagulate the cream proteins. It became a restaurant dessert staple in Britain in the 1990s and 2000s.

recipe.batchNotes: Make up to three days ahead. Cover and refrigerate. Serve direct from the fridge.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Must be made at least 2 hours ahead. Can be refrigerated for up to three days.

content.text: The setting mechanism here is not gelatine but acid: the lemon juice causes the proteins in the double cream to tighten and set. This only works with double cream, which has the fat content needed to set properly. Single cream or whipping cream won't set the same way. The lemon juice must be fresh, bottled juice can be inconsistent and affects the flavour.

The cream mixture must be hot, not warm, but properly hot, before the lemon juice is added. Cold or lukewarm cream won't set. Pour the lemon juice in while stirring, then pour immediately into glasses or ramekins before the mixture starts to thicken in the pan.

items.prepNote: finely grated zest and juice (about 100 ml juice)

content.text: Six small ramekins or glasses, 100, 120 ml capacity. Tall, narrow glasses show the set cream off better than wide, shallow ramekins.

Put {{double-cream}} of double cream, {{caster-sugar}} of caster sugar, and the lemon zest into a medium saucepan. Bring to the boil over medium heat, stirring to dissolve the sugar. Once at a full boil, let it boil for 2 minutes, watch the pan, as the cream rises fast. Remove from the heat.

Strain the lemon juice through a fine sieve to remove any pips or pulp. Stir the hot cream mixture, then pour in the strained lemon juice while stirring. The mixture will thicken slightly. Pour immediately through a sieve into each glass or ramekin, working quickly before it sets in the pan.

Leave to cool at room temperature for 20 minutes, then transfer to the fridge for at least 2 hours until set. Serve cold, straight from the fridge, topped with a little fresh fruit or a dusting of lemon zest.

items.symptom: Posset didn't set and is still liquid after 2 hours
items.cause: Cream was not hot enough when the lemon juice was added, or not enough lemon juice was used
items.fix: Make sure the cream is at a full boil, not just steaming, before adding the juice. Measure the lemon juice: about 100 ml from 3 lemons is needed for 600 ml of cream.

items.symptom: Posset is grainy or curdled in texture
items.cause: Cream boiled for too long or the lemon juice was added too slowly
items.fix: Boil for no more than 2 minutes. Add the lemon juice quickly while stirring. Straining through a sieve when pouring into glasses will catch any lumps.

items.symptom: Flavour is very sharp and sour
items.cause: Too much lemon juice, or the lemons were particularly acidic
items.fix: Use 90 ml of juice for a milder flavour. Taste the lemon before starting, a very acidic lemon may need only 2.5 lemons.

items.symptom: Surface has a slightly yellow, skin-like layer
items.cause: Posset was left to cool uncovered and a skin formed
items.fix: Cover each glass or ramekin with cling film while cooling and refrigerating. The skin can be lifted off if it has formed.

content.text: The posset has a long history in British cooking, originally as a hot milk drink curdled with wine or ale and taken medicinally from the medieval period onward. The cold lemon posset, in which the acid sets the cream rather than curdling it into a drink, is a much more recent development, associated with British restaurant cooking from the late twentieth century. It became popular as a restaurant dessert because it could be made in advance, scaled easily, and produced a reliable result. The same basic technique appears occasionally in older British cookery as a lemon cream, though the name was not consistently applied.
